subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted with community-acquired pneumonia and started on amoxicillin. The attending physician explains the mechanism of action of beta-lactam antibiotics to medical students. Which of the following correctly describes the primary target of beta-lactam antibiotics?
options: Inhibition of DNA gyrase enzyme
Binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) and inhibiting transpeptidase activity
Disruption of the 30S ribosomal subunit
Inhibition of D-Ala-D-Ala terminal peptide synthesis
explanation: Beta-lactam antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems) act by binding covalently to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), which are enzymes (transpeptidases) responsible for cross-linking peptidoglycan chains in the bacterial cell wall. This inhibition prevents cell wall synthesis, leading to cell lysis and death. DNA gyrase is the target of fluoroquinolones. 30S ribosome is targeted by aminoglycosides/tetracyclines. D-Ala-D-Ala is the target of vancomycin (not beta-lactams).
highYield: Classic NEET PG distinction: beta-lactams inhibit transpeptidase (PBP), while vancomycin binds D-Ala-D-Ala. Do not confuse these two mechanisms as both affect cell wall synthesis.
pyqNote: PBP/transpeptidase inhibition as beta-lactam MOA is a direct recall question that has appeared in NEET PG and AIIMS PG examinations. The distinction from vancomycin MOA is frequently tested.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 30-year-old intravenous drug user presents with a right-sided endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus that is methicillin-sensitive (MSSA) and penicillinase-producing. Which of the following penicillins would be MOST appropriate to use?
options: Ampicillin
Piperacillin
Cloxacillin
Benzylpenicillin
explanation: Cloxacillin (along with oxacillin, nafcillin, dicloxacillin, and flucloxacillin) is a penicillinase-resistant (beta-lactamase-resistant) penicillin. These agents have a bulky side chain that sterically hinders beta-lactamase from cleaving the beta-lactam ring. They are specifically used for MSSA infections. Ampicillin and piperacillin are penicillinase-sensitive (though piperacillin/tazobactam combination provides coverage). Benzylpenicillin is highly susceptible to beta-lactamases. For MRSA, none of these would be effective — vancomycin is required.
highYield: Penicillinase-resistant penicillins: Cloxacillin, Oxacillin, Nafcillin, Dicloxacillin, Flucloxacillin. Mnemonic: CONDF — used for MSSA only.
pyqNote: Frequently tested in NEET PG: choosing the correct penicillin for MSSA vs MRSA. Cloxacillin/oxacillin/nafcillin are DOC for MSSA; vancomycin for MRSA.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A culture from a wound infection grows Staphylococcus aureus that is resistant to oxacillin. Genetic analysis reveals resistance is mediated by the mecA gene. What is the mechanism by which mecA confers methicillin resistance?
options: Production of a beta-lactamase enzyme that hydrolyzes the beta-lactam ring
Efflux pump overexpression leading to reduced intracellular antibiotic concentration
Acquisition of an altered penicillin-binding protein (PBP2a) with low affinity for beta-lactams
Thickening of the peptidoglycan cell wall preventing antibiotic penetration
explanation: The mecA gene encodes PBP2a (also called PBP2'), an altered penicillin-binding protein with very low affinity for all beta-lactam antibiotics including methicillin, oxacillin, and cloxacillin. Because the bacteria can still perform transpeptidation via PBP2a even in the presence of beta-lactams, the bacteria survive. This is a qualitative resistance mechanism (altered target) — distinct from beta-lactamase production (enzymatic destruction). This is why all beta-lactams (including carbapenems) are ineffective against MRSA, and vancomycin is the drug of choice.
highYield: MRSA resistance = mecA gene → PBP2a (low affinity for all beta-lactams). This is target modification, not enzyme production. Vancomycin is DOC for MRSA infections.
pyqNote: The mecA/PBP2a mechanism is a high-yield NEET PG topic. Know the distinction: beta-lactamase (enzymatic, plasmid-mediated, inhibited by clavulanate) vs PBP2a (target modification, chromosomal, not inhibited by beta-lactamase inhibitors).

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 65-year-old diabetic woman develops a urinary tract infection with E. coli that tests positive for extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) production. She has no prior antibiotic allergies. Which of the following is the drug of choice for treating a serious systemic infection caused by ESBL-producing organisms?
options: Cefotaxime
Piperacillin-tazobactam
Meropenem
Ciprofloxacin
explanation: Carbapenems (imipenem, meropenem, ertapenem) are the drugs of choice for serious infections caused by ESBL-producing organisms. ESBLs (most commonly CTX-M type in India and globally) are enzymes that hydrolyze extended-spectrum cephalosporins (including cefotaxime, ceftriaxone, ceftazidime) and penicillins, but are inhibited by carbapenems. Piperacillin-tazobactam is theoretically active but clinically unreliable for serious ESBL infections. Ciprofloxacin resistance often co-exists with ESBL production. The most common ESBL worldwide is CTX-M (formerly TEM and SHV were more common).
highYield: ESBL: CTX-M is most common globally; hydrolyzes 3rd-gen cephalosporins; DOC = carbapenem. Detected by double-disk synergy test (DDST).
pyqNote: ESBL treatment is a classic NEET PG question. Remember: CTX-M is most common ESBL; carbapenems are DOC; ESBL organisms can cause treatment failures even when in vitro sensitivity to pip-tazo is shown.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 55-year-old ICU patient on mechanical ventilation develops hospital-acquired pneumonia with Klebsiella pneumoniae that produces NDM-1 (New Delhi Metallo-beta-lactamase). Which of the following treatment options is MOST appropriate?
options: Meropenem monotherapy
Ceftriaxone
Ceftazidime-avibactam
Ampicillin-sulbactam
explanation: NDM-1 is a metallo-beta-lactamase (MBL) that confers resistance to all carbapenems and most other beta-lactams. For carbapenem-resistant organisms producing serine carbapenemases (KPC, OXA-48), ceftazidime-avibactam is effective because avibactam inhibits serine beta-lactamases including KPC. However, avibactam does NOT inhibit metallo-beta-lactamases like NDM. For NDM-producing organisms, the options include aztreonam-avibactam (aztreonam is resistant to MBLs; avibactam protects it from co-existing serine beta-lactamases), polymyxins (colistin, polymyxin B), or combination therapy. Ceftazidime-avibactam is active against KPC/OXA-48 but limited against NDM alone. In practice, polymyxins with combination regimens are used for NDM producers.
highYield: Carbapenem resistance mechanisms: NDM/VIM/IMP (metallo-beta-lactamases — not inhibited by avibactam), KPC/OXA-48 (serine carbapenemases — inhibited by avibactam). Treatment: colistin/polymyxin for NDM; ceftazidime-avibactam for KPC.
pyqNote: NDM-1 was first reported from India (New Delhi). It confers pan-drug resistance. The distinction between MBL (NDM) vs serine carbapenemase (KPC) and appropriate treatment is a high-yield NEET PG concept.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 40-year-old patient with MRSA bacteremia is started on vancomycin infusion. During rapid intravenous infusion, the patient develops facial flushing, erythema over the neck and upper chest, and pruritus. Which of the following best explains this reaction?
options: IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity allergic reaction
Non-immunologic histamine release from mast cells
Complement activation by the classical pathway
Type III immune complex-mediated hypersensitivity
explanation: The described reaction is Red Man Syndrome (RMS), a non-immunologic adverse effect of vancomycin caused by direct (non-IgE-mediated) histamine release from mast cells and basophils when the drug is infused too rapidly. It presents with flushing, erythema, and pruritus predominantly over the face, neck, and upper thorax (the 'blush area'). It is NOT a true allergic reaction (not IgE-mediated). Prevention involves slowing the infusion rate (infuse over at least 60 minutes) and/or pretreatment with antihistamines. This distinction is critical — red man syndrome does not contraindicate future vancomycin use, unlike true anaphylaxis.
highYield: Red Man Syndrome = histamine release (NOT allergy, NOT IgE) due to rapid vancomycin infusion. Prevent by slow infusion. Other vancomycin toxicities: nephrotoxicity (especially with aminoglycosides) and ototoxicity.
pyqNote: Red Man Syndrome is a frequently asked NEET PG question. The key distinction is that it is NOT an allergic (IgE-mediated) reaction — it is a pharmacologic effect due to histamine release. Treatment = slow infusion + antihistamines.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Vancomycin is effective against MRSA because it acts by a mechanism completely different from beta-lactam antibiotics. What is the correct mechanism of action of vancomycin?
options: Inhibits transpeptidase (PBP) cross-linking of peptidoglycan
Binds to D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, preventing cross-linking
Inhibits beta-lactamase enzymes produced by bacteria
Blocks translocation step at 50S ribosomal subunit
explanation: Vancomycin acts by binding to the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of the peptidoglycan precursor (Lipid II), physically blocking transglycosylase and transpeptidase reactions required for cell wall synthesis. This steric inhibition is completely different from beta-lactams which covalently bind to PBPs (transpeptidases). Because the target is different, vancomycin retains activity against MRSA (which has PBP2a resistant to beta-lactams). VRE (Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus) resistance occurs because of modification of D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac, reducing vancomycin binding affinity by 1000-fold.
highYield: Vancomycin: binds D-Ala-D-Ala (NOT PBP). This is the key distinction from beta-lactams. Vancomycin is DOC for MRSA. VRE resistance due to D-Ala-D-Lac substitution.
pyqNote: A classic NEET PG question. Many students incorrectly state vancomycin inhibits PBP — it does NOT. It binds D-Ala-D-Ala to block cell wall synthesis by a different steric mechanism.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 60-year-old patient on gentamicin therapy for gram-negative sepsis complains of progressive hearing loss and tinnitus. Audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss. Which of the following best describes the ototoxicity of aminoglycosides?
options: Reversible cochlear damage that resolves after drug discontinuation
Reversible vestibular toxicity with permanent cochlear damage
Irreversible cochlear and vestibular toxicity due to destruction of hair cells
Conductive hearing loss due to middle ear effusion
explanation: Aminoglycoside ototoxicity is characteristically irreversible because the drugs cause destruction of the sensory hair cells (outer hair cells) of the cochlea (cochlear toxicity — high-frequency hearing loss first) and vestibular apparatus (vestibular toxicity — vertigo, ataxia). Hair cells cannot regenerate in mammals. Neomycin is most ototoxic, followed by gentamicin (more vestibulotoxic), streptomycin (more vestibulotoxic), and tobramycin. The toxicity is concentration-dependent and accumulates in perilymph. Monitoring with audiometry and drug levels is essential. Nephrotoxicity of aminoglycosides is tubular in nature and usually reversible.
highYield: Aminoglycoside ototoxicity: IRREVERSIBLE (hair cell destruction). Cochlear (hearing loss, starts at high frequencies) + vestibular (vertigo, ataxia). Neomycin most ototoxic. Nephrotoxicity is reversible.
pyqNote: The irreversibility of aminoglycoside ototoxicity is a consistently tested fact in NEET PG. Contrast with vancomycin ototoxicity (also possible, usually reversible with dose adjustment). Gentamicin: both cochlear and vestibular toxicity.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Aminoglycosides differ from tetracyclines in that they are bactericidal despite both targeting the 30S ribosomal subunit. Which of the following properties BEST explains why aminoglycosides are bactericidal rather than bacteriostatic?
options: Aminoglycosides cause irreversible misreading of mRNA leading to aberrant protein synthesis and membrane disruption
Aminoglycosides have higher lipid solubility allowing better cell wall penetration
Aminoglycosides inhibit initiation complex formation more effectively than tetracyclines
Aminoglycosides chelate calcium ions in the bacterial membrane
explanation: Aminoglycosides bind irreversibly to the 30S ribosomal subunit (specifically the 16S rRNA), causing misreading of mRNA codons. This leads to incorporation of wrong amino acids, producing aberrant/nonfunctional proteins that insert into the cell membrane, causing membrane disruption and increased permeability. This creates a 'self-perpetuating uptake' cycle — more drug enters, causing more membrane damage and more drug entry — ultimately causing cell death (bactericidal). Tetracyclines also bind 30S but reversibly inhibit aminoacyl-tRNA binding without causing membrane disruption, hence bacteriostatic. Aminoglycosides also exhibit a significant post-antibiotic effect (PAE), allowing once-daily dosing.
highYield: Aminoglycosides: 30S ribosome, bactericidal (membrane disruption via aberrant proteins), concentration-dependent killing, significant PAE (allows once-daily dosing). Tetracyclines: 30S, bacteriostatic.
pyqNote: The bactericidal mechanism of aminoglycosides despite 30S target (same as tetracyclines) is a conceptual NEET PG question. The post-antibiotic effect of aminoglycosides enabling once-daily dosing is also tested.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 55-year-old immunocompromised patient presents with gram-negative septicemia. Blood cultures are pending. Which aminoglycoside is considered the drug of choice for empirical treatment of serious gram-negative infections including Pseudomonas aeruginosa?
options: Neomycin
Streptomycin
Gentamicin
Spectinomycin
explanation: Gentamicin is the drug of choice for serious gram-negative septicemia, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections, typically used in combination with beta-lactams (synergistic). Neomycin is too toxic for systemic use and is only used topically or as pre-operative bowel decontamination. Streptomycin is used for tuberculosis (1st-line injectable) and plague/tularemia. Spectinomycin is used specifically for gonorrhea (in penicillin-allergic patients) and does not cover Pseudomonas. Amikacin is used when gentamicin resistance is encountered (broader resistance coverage).
highYield: Gentamicin = DOC for gram-negative sepsis including Pseudomonas (combine with beta-lactam). Neomycin = topical only (most toxic systemically). Streptomycin = TB + plague. Amikacin = gentamicin-resistant gram-negatives.
pyqNote: Clinical applications of specific aminoglycosides are frequently tested. Gentamicin as DOC for gram-negative sepsis is a direct recall question in NEET PG.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A pregnant woman in her second trimester is prescribed doxycycline for a chlamydial infection. Which of the following is the primary reason tetracyclines are contraindicated in pregnancy and young children under 8 years?
options: Teratogenic cardiac malformations in the first trimester
Chelation with calcium causing teeth discoloration and bone growth inhibition
Hepatotoxicity leading to acute fatty liver in pregnancy
Nephrotoxicity worsened by pregnancy-induced physiologic changes
explanation: Tetracyclines chelate calcium ions and deposit in calcifying tissues (teeth and bones). In children under 8 years and fetuses, this causes: (1) permanent yellowish-brown discoloration (staining) of primary and secondary teeth (teeth discoloration), and (2) inhibition of bone growth. In pregnancy, tetracyclines are contraindicated as they cross the placenta and damage developing fetal teeth and bones. Additionally, tetracyclines can cause hepatotoxicity (especially with high doses IV in pregnancy) and esophageal ulceration (particularly doxycycline — must be taken with plenty of water, upright). Alternatives for chlamydia in pregnancy: azithromycin (safe in pregnancy).
highYield: Tetracyclines CI in: children <8 years, pregnancy (calcium chelation → teeth staining + bone growth inhibition). Also: esophageal ulceration, photosensitivity, hepatotoxicity. Safe alternative in pregnancy: azithromycin.
pyqNote: Teeth staining and CI in pregnancy/children <8 years for tetracyclines is a mandatory NEET PG recall. Also important: Fanconi syndrome with outdated tetracyclines (degradation product).

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 28-year-old traveler returning from the southeastern United States presents with fever, headache, and a petechial rash starting at the wrists and spreading centrally. He was recently in a tick-infested area. Which antibiotic is the drug of choice for this condition?
options: Azithromycin
Doxycycline
Ciprofloxacin
Chloramphenicol
explanation: The clinical picture (traveler, tick exposure, petechial rash starting peripherally and spreading centripetally, fever, headache) is classic for Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF) caused by Rickettsia rickettsii. Doxycycline is the drug of choice for RMSF in all patients including children (the severity of RMSF outweighs the risk of teeth staining). Doxycycline is also DOC for: Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi), Chlamydia infections, Mycoplasma pneumonia, Q fever (Coxiella burnetii), brucellosis (with rifampicin), cholera, and atypical pneumonias. Chloramphenicol was the older alternative for RMSF but is now reserved due to aplastic anemia risk.
highYield: Doxycycline DOC for: RMSF, Lyme disease, Chlamydia, atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma/Chlamydophila), Rickettsia, Q fever, brucellosis, cholera. Even used in children with RMSF (severity overrides teeth staining risk).
pyqNote: Doxycycline as DOC for rickettsia/RMSF is a classic NEET PG question. The clinical scenario of tick bite + petechial rash starting at wrists is the classic RMSF vignette.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 35-year-old patient on erythromycin for community-acquired pneumonia develops palpitations. ECG shows a prolonged QTc interval. Which of the following best explains this finding, and which additional concern is clinically significant with erythromycin?
options: Sodium channel blockade; erythromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inducer increasing metabolism of other drugs
Potassium channel (HERG) blockade causing QT prolongation; erythromycin is a CYP3A4 inhibitor increasing levels of co-administered drugs
Direct myocardial depression; erythromycin inhibits CYP2D6
Calcium channel blockade; erythromycin inhibits P-glycoprotein only
explanation: Erythromycin prolongs the QT interval by blocking cardiac HERG (hERG) potassium channels (IKr), which can precipitate torsades de pointes — a potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmia. Additionally, erythromycin is a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4 (cytochrome P450 3A4), significantly increasing plasma levels of co-administered drugs metabolized by CYP3A4 (e.g., statins → rhabdomyolysis, warfarin → bleeding, cyclosporine → nephrotoxicity, theophylline → toxicity, terfenadine → fatal arrhythmia). Azithromycin has less CYP3A4 inhibition and less QT effect. Clarithromycin also inhibits CYP3A4 but is less cardiotoxic than erythromycin.
highYield: Erythromycin: QT prolongation (HERG/IKr block) + CYP3A4 inhibitor (major drug interactions). Azithromycin: long t1/2 (~68h), high tissue concentrations, minimal CYP interactions. Clarithromycin: H. pylori triple therapy.
pyqNote: Erythromycin as a CYP3A4 inhibitor causing drug interactions AND QT prolongation are both high-yield NEET PG facts. The distinction between erythromycin (enzyme inhibitor) and rifampicin (enzyme inducer) is commonly tested.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Azithromycin is preferred over erythromycin for outpatient treatment of atypical pneumonia due to its superior pharmacokinetic profile. Which of the following BEST describes azithromycin's pharmacokinetic advantage?
options: Shorter half-life allowing more frequent dosing for better compliance
Very long half-life (~68 hours) with high tissue concentrations allowing 3-5 day treatment courses
Superior CYP3A4 inhibition preventing drug interactions
Better oral bioavailability (>90%) compared to other macrolides
explanation: Azithromycin has an exceptionally long plasma half-life of approximately 68 hours (some tissues retain it for 5-7 days) and achieves very high concentrations in tissues (tissue:plasma ratio up to 100:1) and phagocytes (macrophages carry it to sites of infection). This allows for short course therapy (e.g., 3-5 days) that maintains therapeutic tissue concentrations for 7-10 days. This makes it ideal for once-daily dosing and short courses (Z-pack = 5 days). Azithromycin has relatively low oral bioavailability (~37%), but this is compensated by excellent tissue distribution. It has minimal CYP3A4 inhibition (less drug interactions than erythromycin).
highYield: Azithromycin: long t1/2 (~68h), high tissue concentrations (phagocyte delivery), minimal CYP3A4 effects, short treatment course (3-5 days), once-daily dosing. Also safe in pregnancy (alternative to doxycycline for chlamydia).
pyqNote: The pharmacokinetic basis of azithromycin's 3-5 day course effectiveness is frequently tested in NEET PG. The long half-life and tissue accumulation are key distinguishing features from erythromycin.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 45-year-old patient with confirmed H. pylori infection and peptic ulcer disease is prescribed triple therapy. Which of the following combinations constitutes the standard first-line triple therapy for H. pylori eradication?
options: Amoxicillin + Metronidazole + Omeprazole
Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin + Proton pump inhibitor
Doxycycline + Bismuth + Omeprazole
Azithromycin + Metronidazole + Ranitidine
explanation: Standard first-line triple therapy for H. pylori eradication: Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin (or Metronidazole) + PPI (e.g., omeprazole, lansoprazole) for 7-14 days. Clarithromycin-based triple therapy remains the standard first-line regimen where clarithromycin resistance rates are <15-20%. Second-line (bismuth quadruple therapy): Bismuth + Metronidazole + Tetracycline + PPI. Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and can interact with drugs, but it achieves excellent mucosal concentrations necessary for H. pylori eradication.
highYield: H. pylori triple therapy: PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin (or metronidazole) × 14 days. Bismuth quadruple = second-line/clarithromycin-resistant areas. Clarithromycin: most commonly used macrolide for H. pylori.
pyqNote: H. pylori triple therapy combination is a core NEET PG question. Know which macrolide is used (clarithromycin, NOT azithromycin or erythromycin) and the complete combination.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Fluoroquinolones exert their bactericidal effect through inhibition of which bacterial enzyme(s)?
options: RNA polymerase (beta subunit) in gram-positive organisms and transcriptase in gram-negative organisms
DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) primarily in gram-negative bacteria and topoisomerase IV primarily in gram-positive bacteria
Thymidylate synthase in gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria equally
Dihydropteroate synthase in gram-negative and topoisomerase I in gram-positive bacteria
explanation: Fluoroquinolones inhibit two bacterial type II topoisomerases: (1) DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) — primary target in gram-negative bacteria, involved in negative supercoiling of DNA during replication; and (2) Topoisomerase IV — primary target in gram-positive bacteria, involved in decatenation (separation) of daughter chromosomes after replication. Inhibition traps the enzyme-DNA cleavage complex, leading to DNA strand breaks and bacterial death (bactericidal). RNA polymerase is the target of rifampicin. Dihydropteroate synthase is the target of sulfonamides.
highYield: Fluoroquinolones: DNA gyrase (primary in gram-negatives) + Topoisomerase IV (primary in gram-positives). Both are type II topoisomerases. Bactericidal, concentration-dependent.
pyqNote: The dual target (DNA gyrase for gram-negatives, topoisomerase IV for gram-positives) of fluoroquinolones is a high-yield distinction tested in NEET PG. Resistance develops when both targets are mutated.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 16-year-old athlete develops right Achilles tendon rupture while on ciprofloxacin for a urinary tract infection. The orthopedic surgeon asks about the mechanism of fluoroquinolone-induced tendinopathy. Which of the following is CORRECT regarding fluoroquinolone adverse effects?
options: Tendon rupture occurs only in elderly patients over 60 years of age
Fluoroquinolones are safe to use in children and pregnancy as tendon rupture risk is theoretical
Fluoroquinolones can cause tendon rupture (especially Achilles) and are contraindicated in children and pregnancy due to arthropathy in weight-bearing joints
The tendon rupture risk is limited to intravenous formulations of fluoroquinolones
explanation: Fluoroquinolones can cause tendinitis and tendon rupture (most commonly Achilles tendon), which can occur during or after treatment. Risk is increased in patients >60 years, corticosteroid users, and kidney/heart/lung transplant recipients. Additionally, fluoroquinolones are contraindicated in children (because animal studies show irreversible arthropathy/cartilage erosion in weight-bearing joints) and in pregnancy (same reason — fetal cartilage damage). Other adverse effects: prolonged QT interval (especially moxifloxacin, sparfloxacin), CNS effects (seizures, dizziness), photosensitivity, and excellent oral bioavailability (~90%) allowing oral-to-IV switch. Ciprofloxacin is the drug of choice for Pseudomonas aeruginosa among oral antibiotics.
highYield: Fluoroquinolone ADRs: tendon rupture (Achilles), CI in children/pregnancy (cartilage damage), QT prolongation (moxifloxacin > ciprofloxacin). Ciprofloxacin = best oral coverage for Pseudomonas.
pyqNote: Tendon rupture and CI in children/pregnancy for fluoroquinolones are classic NEET PG adverse effect questions. Also know that fluoroquinolones have excellent oral bioavailability (~90%), making them suitable for oral-to-IV switch.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 50-year-old man with a lower respiratory tract infection growing Pseudomonas aeruginosa on culture requires oral antibiotic therapy after discharge. Which fluoroquinolone provides the best oral coverage against Pseudomonas aeruginosa?
options: Moxifloxacin
Levofloxacin
Ciprofloxacin
Gatifloxacin
explanation: Ciprofloxacin is the fluoroquinolone with the best gram-negative activity, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It has excellent oral bioavailability (~70-80%) making it the preferred oral option for Pseudomonas infections (e.g., UTI, respiratory infections in cystic fibrosis patients). Moxifloxacin is a 4th-generation fluoroquinolone with enhanced gram-positive and anaerobic activity but POOR Pseudomonas coverage and should NOT be used for Pseudomonas. Levofloxacin has some Pseudomonas activity but less than ciprofloxacin. Gatifloxacin is associated with glucose metabolism disturbances.
highYield: Ciprofloxacin = best fluoroquinolone for Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Moxifloxacin = gram-positive/anaerobes but NOT Pseudomonas. Always pair fluoroquinolone with beta-lactam for severe Pseudomonas infections.
pyqNote: The distinction between ciprofloxacin (Pseudomonas coverage) and moxifloxacin (no Pseudomonas, but better gram-positive and anaerobic) is a direct NEET PG question.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 32-year-old woman is prescribed metronidazole for bacterial vaginosis. She attends a wedding and consumes alcohol the same evening. Shortly after, she develops severe nausea, vomiting, flushing, tachycardia, and headache. What is the mechanism of this reaction?
options: Metronidazole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase causing methanol accumulation
Metronidazole inhibits acetaldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH2) causing acetaldehyde accumulation — disulfiram-like reaction
Metronidazole competes with alcohol for CYP2E1-mediated metabolism
Alcohol induces CYP3A4, increasing metronidazole metabolism to toxic metabolites
explanation: Metronidazole inhibits acetaldehyde dehydrogenase (aldehyde dehydrogenase), the enzyme responsible for converting acetaldehyde to acetate. When alcohol is consumed, it is metabolized to acetaldehyde by alcohol dehydrogenase. Normally acetaldehyde is rapidly cleared by ALDH2; if this enzyme is inhibited, acetaldehyde accumulates, causing the disulfiram-like reaction: flushing, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, palpitations, and hypotension. This is the same mechanism as disulfiram (Antabuse). Patients must be warned to avoid alcohol during treatment AND for 48 hours after completing metronidazole. Other side effects: metallic taste, peripheral neuropathy (with prolonged use), and neurotoxicity (encephalopathy).
highYield: Metronidazole + alcohol = disulfiram-like reaction (ALDH2 inhibition → acetaldehyde accumulation). Avoid alcohol during treatment and 48h after. Also: metallic taste, peripheral neuropathy, neurotoxicity.
pyqNote: The disulfiram-like reaction of metronidazole with alcohol is a high-frequency NEET PG question. Know the mechanism (ALDH2/acetaldehyde dehydrogenase inhibition) and clinical presentation.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Metronidazole is active against anaerobic bacteria and protozoa. Which of the following correctly describes its mechanism of action at the molecular level?
options: Binds to ergosterol in fungal membranes causing pore formation
Reduced intracellularly to nitro radical anion that causes DNA strand breaks
Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase preventing nucleotide synthesis
Disrupts the 50S ribosome preventing peptide bond formation
explanation: Metronidazole is a prodrug that requires activation under anaerobic (low redox potential) conditions. Intracellularly, electron transport proteins (ferredoxin in protozoa, pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase in bacteria) reduce the nitro group of metronidazole to a cytotoxic nitro radical anion. This radical anion intercalates DNA and causes strand breaks and helix destabilization, ultimately leading to cell death. This mechanism explains why metronidazole is ONLY active against: (1) strict anaerobic bacteria (Bacteroides fragilis, Clostridium), and (2) certain protozoa (Entamoeba histolytica, Giardia lamblia, Trichomonas vaginalis). Aerobic bacteria cannot activate the drug (they maintain a high redox potential).
highYield: Metronidazole MOA: prodrug activated by anaerobic conditions → nitro radical anion → DNA strand breaks. Active against: strict anaerobes + protozoa (Entamoeba, Giardia, Trichomonas). Not effective in aerobic conditions.
pyqNote: The prodrug activation mechanism of metronidazole (requiring low redox potential) explains its selective anaerobic spectrum. This is a conceptual NEET PG question. Metronidazole is DOC for C. difficile (oral), amoebiasis, giardiasis, trichomoniasis, and bacterial vaginosis.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A patient with pulmonary tuberculosis is started on rifampicin monotherapy due to intolerance to other anti-tubercular drugs. Two months later, the patient's sputum culture shows resistance to rifampicin. What is the primary mechanism of action of rifampicin, and why does monotherapy rapidly lead to resistance?
options: Inhibits DNA gyrase; monotherapy selects for topoisomerase mutations at low frequency
Inhibits bacterial RNA polymerase (beta subunit); monotherapy rapidly selects for spontaneous mutations in rpoB gene
Inhibits 23S rRNA methyltransferase; spontaneous mutations in the 23S rRNA are frequent
Inhibits cell wall synthesis at mycolic acid level; frequent spontaneous mutations occur in inhA gene
explanation: Rifampicin specifically inhibits bacterial RNA polymerase by binding to the beta-subunit (encoded by rpoB gene), blocking DNA-dependent RNA synthesis at the initiation stage. Spontaneous mutations in rpoB occur at a frequency of approximately 10^-8 per cell division. In the large bacterial populations present in tuberculosis lesions (10^6-10^9 bacilli), spontaneous rpoB mutants exist. Rifampicin monotherapy rapidly selects for these pre-existing mutants, leading to resistance within weeks. This is why rifampicin is ALWAYS used in combination with at least 3 other drugs in TB treatment. DNA gyrase is the target of fluoroquinolones.
highYield: Rifampicin: inhibits RNA polymerase (beta subunit, rpoB). NEVER use as monotherapy (rapid resistance). Always combine. Also: powerful CYP450 enzyme inducer (major drug interactions), orange/red coloration of secretions, hepatotoxic.
pyqNote: Rifampicin's RNA polymerase inhibition and the requirement for combination therapy (rapid resistance with monotherapy) are essential NEET PG facts. The rpoB gene mutation as resistance mechanism is also tested.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 30-year-old HIV-positive woman on antiretroviral therapy (specifically including a protease inhibitor) is also being treated for tuberculosis. The prescribing physician is concerned about drug interactions. Which property of rifampicin is responsible for the most significant drug interactions?
options: Potent inhibition of CYP3A4 and CYP2C9
Potent induction of multiple CYP450 enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2C19) and P-glycoprotein
Inhibition of glucuronidation by UGT1A1
Competitive displacement of other drugs from plasma protein binding sites
explanation: Rifampicin is one of the most potent known inducers of cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP1A2) as well as P-glycoprotein. By inducing these enzymes, rifampicin dramatically increases the metabolism of co-administered drugs, reducing their plasma levels and therapeutic efficacy. Clinically important interactions include: (1) HIV protease inhibitors (levels drop dramatically — rifabutin preferred instead of rifampicin in HIV-TB co-infection), (2) oral contraceptives (contraceptive failure — a very important warning), (3) warfarin (reduced anticoagulation), (4) corticosteroids, cyclosporine, antifungals, antiepileptics. Side effects: orange coloration of urine/tears/sweat (harmless), hepatotoxicity, flu-like syndrome (intermittent dosing).
highYield: Rifampicin = potent CYP450 INDUCER (CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19) → reduces efficacy of co-medications. Key interactions: OCP failure, reduced protease inhibitor levels, warfarin resistance. Use rifabutin in HIV-TB co-infection.
pyqNote: Rifampicin as enzyme inducer (not inhibitor) is a critical NEET PG distinction. Erythromycin/clarithromycin = enzyme inhibitors. Rifampicin = enzyme inducer. The OCP failure and HIV drug interaction are high-yield clinical points.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 3-week-old premature infant in the neonatal ICU develops circulatory collapse, hypothermia, abdominal distension, and vomiting after receiving chloramphenicol for a suspected bacterial infection. The attending physician suspects 'Grey Baby Syndrome'. What is the underlying mechanism?
options: Chloramphenicol inhibits fetal hemoglobin production causing severe anemia
Immature hepatic UDP-glucuronosyltransferase cannot conjugate chloramphenicol; renal excretion of unconjugated drug is also immature, causing toxic drug accumulation
Chloramphenicol causes hemolysis in G6PD-deficient neonates
Chloramphenicol blocks cardiac sodium channels in neonates who lack protective plasma proteins
explanation: Grey Baby Syndrome occurs in neonates (especially premature) given chloramphenicol. Neonates have immature hepatic glucuronyl transferase (UDP-glucuronosyltransferase) activity, unable to conjugate and inactivate chloramphenicol at normal adult rates. Additionally, renal excretion of free (unconjugated) chloramphenicol is also poor in neonates. This leads to toxic accumulation of free chloramphenicol, causing mitochondrial toxicity and circulatory failure. Clinical features: ashen/grey appearance (cyanosis), hypothermia, flaccidity, abdominal distension, vomiting, circulatory collapse, and high mortality (40%). This is distinct from the two types of bone marrow suppression: (1) dose-dependent, reversible suppression (all patients at high doses), and (2) idiosyncratic aplastic anemia (rare, irreversible, not dose-related, occurs weeks-months later).
highYield: Grey Baby Syndrome: chloramphenicol in neonates → immature glucuronyl transferase → toxic accumulation. Two bone marrow effects: dose-dependent reversible (common) vs idiosyncratic aplastic anemia (rare, irreversible, not dose-dependent).
pyqNote: Grey Baby Syndrome mechanism (immature glucuronidation in neonates) is a classic NEET PG question. The distinction between dose-dependent marrow suppression and idiosyncratic aplastic anemia with chloramphenicol is also frequently tested.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Chloramphenicol is reserved for life-threatening infections due to its serious adverse effects. Which of the following correctly pairs chloramphenicol's mechanism of action with its most feared idiosyncratic adverse effect?
options: Inhibits 30S ribosomal subunit; dose-dependent reversible bone marrow suppression
Inhibits 50S ribosomal subunit (peptidyl transferase); idiosyncratic aplastic anemia (irreversible, not dose-related)
Inhibits RNA polymerase; dose-dependent irreversible pancytopenia
Inhibits cell wall synthesis; immune-mediated aplastic anemia with IgG anti-chloramphenicol antibodies
explanation: Chloramphenicol inhibits the 50S ribosomal subunit by binding to the 23S rRNA and inhibiting the peptidyl transferase reaction (prevents peptide bond formation). It has two distinct types of bone marrow toxicity: (1) Dose-dependent, reversible suppression of all cell lines (occurs with prolonged use/high doses, affects mitochondrial ribosomes in developing red cells — reversible on discontinuation); and (2) Idiosyncratic aplastic anemia — rare (~1 in 20,000-40,000), irreversible, occurs weeks to months after even brief exposure, not dose-related, and has high mortality. The idiosyncratic form is the most feared complication and the primary reason chloramphenicol is reserved for life-threatening indications.
highYield: Chloramphenicol: 50S inhibitor (peptidyl transferase). Two ADRs: (1) dose-dependent reversible marrow suppression, (2) idiosyncratic irreversible aplastic anemia (rare but fatal). Grey Baby Syndrome in neonates.
pyqNote: Chloramphenicol's dual marrow toxicity (dose-dependent reversible vs idiosyncratic irreversible aplastic anemia) is a high-yield NEET PG differentiation question. The 50S target (same as macrolides, linezolid, clindamycin) is also important.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 70-year-old patient with prosthetic valve endocarditis caused by VRE (Vancomycin-Resistant Enterococcus) is started on linezolid. After 3 weeks of therapy, the patient's platelet count drops to 45,000/μL. The patient is also on an SSRI (sertraline) for depression. Which of the following is MOST concerning about this drug combination?
options: Linezolid and sertraline together cause hypokalemia leading to cardiac arrhythmias
Linezolid is a reversible MAO inhibitor; combined with SSRIs it can cause serotonin syndrome
Both drugs inhibit CYP2D6 causing accumulation of each other's metabolites
Linezolid reduces platelet serotonin uptake directly by blocking serotonin transporters
explanation: Linezolid is a reversible, non-selective inhibitor of monoamine oxidase (MAO-A and MAO-B). When combined with serotonergic drugs (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, meperidine, tramadol), it can precipitate serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, agitation, tremor, myoclonus, hyperreflexia, and potentially life-threatening complications. Linezolid should be used with extreme caution (or avoided) with serotonergic drugs. Additional linezolid toxicities include: thrombocytopenia (most common hematologic effect, especially with >2 weeks therapy — as in this case), anemia, peripheral neuropathy, and optic neuropathy. It inhibits the 50S ribosomal subunit and is active against MRSA, VRE, and other resistant gram-positive organisms.
highYield: Linezolid: 50S inhibitor, MAO inhibitor (reversible) → serotonin syndrome with SSRIs/SNRIs, thrombocytopenia (>2 weeks), MRSA+VRE coverage. Never combine with serotonergic agents.
pyqNote: Linezolid's dual risk of serotonin syndrome (with SSRIs) and thrombocytopenia are both high-yield NEET PG adverse effects. The MAO inhibitor property of linezolid is a critical safety consideration.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Linezolid belongs to the oxazolidinone class of antibiotics. Which of the following correctly describes linezolid's unique mechanism of action?
options: Binds to 30S ribosomal subunit, causing mRNA misreading and aberrant protein synthesis
Binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocks formation of the 70S initiation complex by preventing 30S-50S joining
Binds to PBP2a on MRSA, overcoming methicillin resistance by steric displacement
Inhibits peptidyl transferase at the 50S subunit, blocking peptide bond formation
explanation: Linezolid has a unique mechanism that distinguishes it from other 50S inhibitors (chloramphenicol, macrolides, clindamycin): it binds to the 23S rRNA of the 50S subunit and prevents the formation of the 70S initiation complex. Normally, 30S and 50S ribosomal subunits join with mRNA and initiator tRNA to form the 70S initiation complex. Linezolid blocks this initiation step by preventing 30S-50S joining, which is a unique mechanism with minimal cross-resistance to other ribosome-targeted antibiotics. This bacteriostatic activity (against most organisms, including MRSA and VRE) makes it distinct from aminoglycosides (which are bactericidal) even though they target the same ribosomal unit.
highYield: Linezolid: unique MOA = prevents 70S initiation complex formation (blocks 30S-50S joining). This differs from chloramphenicol (peptidyl transferase) and macrolides (translocation). Active against MRSA, VRE, MDR-TB. Bacteriostatic.
pyqNote: Linezolid's unique mechanism of blocking 70S initiation complex formation is an important distinguishing NEET PG fact. Know: linezolid is bacteriostatic (not bactericidal) against MRSA/VRE.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 65-year-old patient with MRSA osteomyelitis is on vancomycin and gentamicin combination therapy. After 10 days, serum creatinine rises from 0.9 to 2.4 mg/dL. Which of the following is TRUE regarding this finding?
options: Nephrotoxicity from this combination is no greater than either drug alone
Combined vancomycin-aminoglycoside use has additive/synergistic nephrotoxicity requiring close renal monitoring
Vancomycin is not nephrotoxic alone; the elevation is entirely due to gentamicin
This rise in creatinine is expected and does not require dose modification
explanation: Vancomycin has intrinsic nephrotoxicity (proximal tubular toxicity), and aminoglycosides (gentamicin) also cause nephrotoxicity (proximal tubular necrosis). When used together, they have additive (and possibly synergistic) nephrotoxic effects, significantly increasing the risk of acute kidney injury. This combination should be used cautiously with close monitoring of renal function, vancomycin trough levels (or AUC-guided monitoring), and gentamicin peak/trough levels. If nephrotoxicity develops, dose adjustment or switch to an alternative (e.g., daptomycin for MRSA in renal impairment) should be considered. Vancomycin nephrotoxicity was historically more common with older formulations containing impurities; modern purified preparations are less nephrotoxic alone but remain concerning in combination.
highYield: Vancomycin + aminoglycosides = additive nephrotoxicity. Monitor creatinine, vancomycin AUC/trough, and gentamicin levels closely. Both are independently nephrotoxic; combination multiplies risk.
pyqNote: The enhanced nephrotoxicity of vancomycin-aminoglycoside combination is a pharmacology safety question frequently seen in NEET PG. Know: vancomycin is nephrotoxic + ototoxic; aminoglycosides are nephrotoxic + ototoxic (irreversible).

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 25-year-old medical student taking doxycycline for acne develops severe retrosternal pain and odynophagia after taking the tablet with a small sip of water and lying down immediately. Upper GI endoscopy reveals an esophageal ulcer. What is the mechanism of this adverse effect and the correct advice to prevent it?
options: Doxycycline directly irritates the gastric mucosa; take with antacids to neutralize acidity
Doxycycline causes local mucosal damage when it dissolves in contact with esophageal mucosa; take upright with a full glass of water, remain upright for 30 minutes
Doxycycline increases gastric acid secretion; take on an empty stomach to speed transit
This is due to iron chelation in the esophagus; avoid iron supplements only
explanation: Doxycycline (and other tetracyclines) can cause esophageal ulceration when the tablet/capsule dissolves in contact with the esophageal mucosa. The mechanism involves local chemical irritation from the highly acidic compound. Prevention: take with a full glass of water (250 mL), remain upright for at least 30 minutes after taking the medication, and avoid taking it immediately before bedtime. This is particularly common in patients who take it at night with minimal water. Antacids should actually be AVOIDED with tetracyclines because divalent cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+) chelate tetracyclines and reduce their absorption. Dairy products, iron supplements, and antacids all reduce tetracycline absorption.
highYield: Doxycycline esophageal ulceration: take with FULL glass of water, remain UPRIGHT. Avoid antacids/dairy/iron (chelation → reduced absorption). This is chemical irritation, NOT acid-related.
pyqNote: Esophageal ulceration and the correct instructions for taking doxycycline are tested in NEET PG. The chelation interaction with divalent cations reducing tetracycline absorption is also a frequently tested concept.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following antibiotics acts on the 50S ribosomal subunit by binding to the 23S rRNA and blocking the translocation step during protein synthesis?
options: Tetracycline
Streptomycin
Erythromycin
Ciprofloxacin
explanation: Macrolides (erythromycin, azithromycin, clarithromycin) bind to the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit and block translocation — the movement of the ribosome along mRNA after peptide bond formation, preventing elongation of the peptide chain. This is a bacteriostatic mechanism. Other 50S inhibitors: chloramphenicol (peptidyl transferase inhibition), linezolid (blocks 70S initiation complex), clindamycin (translocation — same site as macrolides). Tetracyclines and aminoglycosides target the 30S subunit. Ciprofloxacin targets DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV. Streptomycin is an aminoglycoside targeting the 30S subunit (specifically the 16S rRNA at the S12 protein region).
highYield: 50S inhibitors: Macrolides (translocation block), Chloramphenicol (peptidyl transferase), Linezolid (70S initiation), Clindamycin (translocation). 30S inhibitors: Tetracyclines (bacteriostatic), Aminoglycosides (bactericidal).
pyqNote: The ribosomal subunit targets of antibiotics is a high-frequency NEET PG question. Classify: 30S (tetracyclines, aminoglycosides) vs 50S (macrolides, chloramphenicol, linezolid, clindamycin). Macrolides = 50S, translocation block.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 55-year-old diabetic patient with a polymicrobial foot infection is started on piperacillin-tazobactam. What is the role of tazobactam in this combination?
options: Tazobactam enhances penetration of piperacillin into biofilms
Tazobactam is a suicide/irreversible inhibitor of beta-lactamase that protects piperacillin from enzymatic destruction
Tazobactam inhibits efflux pumps that would otherwise expel piperacillin from gram-negative bacteria
Tazobactam competes with piperacillin for PBP binding, creating dual PBP inhibition
explanation: Beta-lactamase inhibitors (clavulanic acid, sulbactam, tazobactam, avibactam) are compounds that inhibit bacterial beta-lactamase enzymes, protecting the companion beta-lactam antibiotic from enzymatic degradation. Clavulanic acid, sulbactam, and tazobactam are 'suicide inhibitors' or 'mechanism-based inhibitors' — they bind irreversibly to serine beta-lactamases, permanently inactivating them. They have minimal intrinsic antibacterial activity alone. Tazobactam is the most potent of the three. Combinations: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin), Ampicillin-Sulbactam, Piperacillin-Tazobactam (Zosyn), Ceftazidime-Avibactam (for carbapenem-resistant organisms with KPC). Important: these combinations do NOT cover MRSA.
highYield: Beta-lactamase inhibitors (clavulanate, sulbactam, tazobactam): suicide inhibitors of serine beta-lactamases. Protect the companion antibiotic, no significant own activity. Do not cover MRSA. Avibactam inhibits ESBL and KPC.
pyqNote: The mechanism of beta-lactamase inhibitors (suicide/irreversible inhibition) and the common combinations (amoxicillin-clavulanate, piperacillin-tazobactam) are frequently tested in NEET PG.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 50-year-old patient on gentamicin therapy for 2 weeks develops oliguria and rising serum creatinine. Urinalysis shows granular casts and proteinuria. Which of the following best describes the mechanism and reversibility of aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity?
options: Glomerular injury due to immune complex deposition; usually irreversible
Proximal tubular cell toxicity due to drug accumulation in renal cortex; usually reversible upon discontinuation
Obstructive nephropathy due to crystal precipitation in collecting ducts; treated with hydration
Interstitial nephritis due to allergic mechanism; treated with corticosteroids
explanation: Aminoglycosides cause nephrotoxicity by accumulating in the proximal tubular cells of the renal cortex (actively taken up by tubular cells). They disrupt lysosomal membranes (phospholipidosis) and mitochondria, causing proximal tubular necrosis. This leads to non-oliguric or oliguric acute kidney injury, typically after 7-10 days of therapy, presenting with rising creatinine, granular casts, and proteinuria. Unlike ototoxicity, aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity is usually reversible upon discontinuation of the drug (tubular cells can regenerate). Risk factors: prolonged therapy, high doses, elderly, pre-existing renal disease, volume depletion, concomitant nephrotoxins (vancomycin, NSAIDs, contrast dyes). Prevention: once-daily dosing (higher peak with prolonged drug-free period), adequate hydration, avoid prolonged use.
highYield: Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity: proximal tubular cell accumulation → necrosis. Reversible (unlike ototoxicity which is irreversible). Non-oliguric AKI. Contrast: ototoxicity (irreversible hair cell destruction).
pyqNote: The reversibility of aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity (vs irreversibility of ototoxicity) is a critical NEET PG distinction. Proximal tubule is the site of renal damage.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A hospitalized patient on intravenous ciprofloxacin for a gram-negative infection is clinically improving. The physician plans to transition to oral ciprofloxacin for discharge. Which property of fluoroquinolones makes this IV-to-oral switch appropriate without compromising therapeutic efficacy?
options: Fluoroquinolones are pro-drugs that must be converted to active forms in the gut
Fluoroquinolones have excellent oral bioavailability (approximately 70-100%) approaching IV levels
Oral ciprofloxacin has higher peak concentrations than IV due to first-pass effect bypass in intestinal cells
Oral fluoroquinolones have longer half-lives than IV formulations, compensating for lower absorption
explanation: Fluoroquinolones have exceptional oral bioavailability of approximately 70-100% (ciprofloxacin ~70-80%, levofloxacin ~99%, moxifloxacin ~90%). This means oral and IV formulations achieve comparable plasma concentrations. This makes IV-to-oral (step-down) therapy with fluoroquinolones appropriate and clinically effective, reducing cost, avoiding IV line complications, and allowing earlier discharge. The only caveat: absorption is reduced by divalent cations (antacids, iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc supplements, dairy) — administer fluoroquinolones 2 hours before or 6 hours after these products. Fluoroquinolones exhibit concentration-dependent bactericidal activity with good tissue penetration.
highYield: Fluoroquinolones: excellent oral bioavailability (~70-99%). IV-to-oral switch is appropriate. Absorption reduced by antacids/calcium/iron/milk (divalent cations). Concentration-dependent killing.
pyqNote: The excellent oral bioavailability of fluoroquinolones enabling seamless IV-to-oral switch is a pharmacokinetic NEET PG question. Also know: antacids reduce quinolone absorption by chelation (same mechanism as tetracyclines).
